Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty scattered. I'd try to set aside time on weekends, but life kept getting in the way—work, family stuff, just general exhaustion. I'd end up cramming in study sessions whenever I could find a spare hour or two. Being 27 and juggling everything, I couldn't stick to a perfect schedule like some younger students might. I mostly listened to lecture recordings while doing other things, which worked better for how I learn anyway.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied a lot depending on what was due. Some weeks I'd barely touch anything, then suddenly I'd realize a deadline was coming and put in long hours. I tried to be more consistent, but competing responsibilities always seemed to win. When work got busy or personal stuff came up, studying was the first thing to slide. It wasn't ideal, but it's how I survived the semester.
Q3: Usually it was a deadline looming or getting an email notification that something was due. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if I'd missed anything important since my attendance wasn't great. I didn't have that habit of checking in daily like some students do. It was more reactive than proactive, if I'm being honest. The gamified task notifications actually surprised me and got me to log in a few extra times.
Q4: Not really, no. I never got to a point where logging into the LMS felt automatic or natural. There were brief periods where I'd check in more regularly, especially when those gamified elements were introduced—that caught my attention for a bit. But it never became a true habit. Life outside of school always pulled my focus elsewhere before any routine could really stick.
Q5: The lecture recordings were the most useful for me because I'm an auditory learner. I could listen to them while commuting or doing chores, which made studying actually possible with my schedule. The discussion boards were occasionally helpful when other students explained things in ways that clicked better than the lectures. I appreciated resources I could access on my own time rather than having to be somewhere specific.
Q6: I pretty much ignored the reading materials and supplementary PDFs. They just sat there, and I kept telling myself I'd get to them later but rarely did. The discussion posts beyond the required ones felt like extra work I didn't have energy for. If something wasn't directly tied to an assignment or exam, it usually fell to the bottom of my priority list. I know that probably hurt my understanding, but there's only so much time.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see a direct connection to passing the course—like if it would help me on an exam or complete an assignment. The gamified tasks were surprisingly engaging because they broke things into smaller chunks and gave me that little dopamine hit of completion. If something felt too abstract or disconnected from what I needed to do to pass, I'd skip it. Practical, immediate relevance was everything for me.
Q8: Yeah, optional activities felt completely skippable to me. Required ones had that external pressure that forced me to engage even when I didn't want to. With optional stuff, I'd think 'that would be nice to do' but then never actually do it because something else always seemed more urgent. The gamified optional tasks were a bit different—they felt more like a game than homework, which made them less intimidating. But still, required always won out over optional.
Q9: Absolutely. I downloaded a lot of the audio lectures and listened to them offline constantly. I also used my own notes from previous courses and some YouTube videos that explained concepts differently. My study time was often in places without reliable internet—on the bus, at my part-time job during breaks. The LMS wouldn't have captured any of that, but it was real studying happening.
Q10: There were many times I was genuinely learning but not leaving any digital footprint. I'd listen to downloaded lectures multiple times, discuss course concepts with a friend who took the class before, and work through problems on paper. Sometimes I'd have the LMS open but be working through things mentally or in a notebook. My actual engagement was way higher than my login frequency would suggest.
Q11: All the time. I'd download the lecture recordings once and then replay them for weeks without logging back in. Same with any PDFs I managed to actually download—I'd save them to my phone and review them during downtime. It was more convenient than constantly logging in, especially when internet was spotty. One download could mean hours of offline studying that the system would never see.
Q12: Mid-semester was brutal. Work picked up, I was dealing with some family stress, and the course material got more complex all at once. My attendance dropped even more than usual, and I felt like I was drowning a bit. The surprise of how quickly things piled up caught me off guard. I had to make tough choices about what to prioritize, and unfortunately the course often lost out to immediate life demands.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted from work, I'd log in but just stare at the screen without really absorbing anything. Confusion about certain topics made me avoid those modules entirely—I'd tell myself I'd figure it out later. Competing responsibilities meant the LMS became something I visited in crisis mode rather than consistently. The fatigue was real; sometimes I'd fall asleep trying to watch lecture videos at night.
Q14: Oh, plenty of times. I'd open videos and let them play while my mind wandered completely elsewhere. Sometimes I'd scroll through materials just to feel like I was doing something productive, but nothing was actually sinking in. There's a difference between having something open and actually learning from it. My task completion numbers probably look better than my actual comprehension was in those moments.
Q15: I postponed the longer reading assignments constantly because they felt overwhelming. Even when I knew they were important for understanding the bigger picture, I'd convince myself I could catch up later. The anxiety of starting something big made avoidance feel easier in the moment. I'd do the quick tasks to feel productive while the important stuff kept getting pushed back. It's not a great strategy, but it's what happened.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my entire motivation system. I'd see a deadline approaching and suddenly become very active on the LMS. Without that external pressure, I'd drift and lose focus on the course entirely. The closer the deadline, the more intense my engagement—but also the more stressed and rushed my work became. It wasn't healthy, but deadlines were the only thing that consistently got me to act.
Q17: Feedback had a mixed effect on me. Good feedback motivated me briefly, but that feeling faded fast. Critical feedback sometimes made me want to disengage because it felt discouraging when I was already struggling to keep up. I appreciated knowing where I stood, but it didn't fundamentally change my patterns. The gamified feedback with points and progress bars was actually more motivating than written comments, surprisingly.
Q18: The course structure felt like it assumed students had more time and consistency than I did. Weekly modules with multiple components were hard to keep up with given my schedule. I appreciated when things were broken into smaller pieces, but the overall pace felt relentless. The structure didn't really accommodate someone like me who was juggling work and life. I had to adapt the structure to my reality rather than the other way around.
Q19: Having audio versions of lectures made engagement much easier for me as an auditory learner. The gamified elements that got introduced were surprisingly effective at pulling me in. What made it harder was when materials were only available in formats that required sitting at a computer—that just didn't fit my life. Also, the discussion requirements felt forced and took time away from actual learning. Flexibility helped; rigidity hurt.
Q20: I think that could be someone who's anxious and checking things repeatedly without actually completing work, or someone who's confused and searching for help but not finding it. It could also be someone dealing with perfectionism who keeps reviewing but can't commit to submitting. Or maybe they're engaged mentally but struggling with the actual execution of assignments. High activity doesn't always mean productive activity.
Q21: That actually sounds like me in some ways. I performed okay despite low visible activity because I did a lot of learning offline and through methods the LMS couldn't track. Some people are efficient—they get what they need and leave. Others might have strong prior knowledge or learn well independently. The LMS only sees one slice of how someone engages with course material. My exam scores came from studying that happened away from the platform.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when someone's active, what they click on, whether they're consistent or cramming. But they miss the quality of engagement, the offline studying, the mental processing that happens away from screens. They can't capture if someone's actually understanding or just going through motions. They also miss life context—why someone's engagement dropped or spiked. Numbers tell a story, but it's an incomplete one.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just checking boxes to pass. It's when you're mentally present and curious, asking questions even if just in your head. For me, it's listening to a lecture and actually thinking about how it connects to things I know. It's not about time spent logged in—it's about whether your brain is actually working on the content. True engagement can happen anywhere.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I learned through listening to downloaded lectures during my commute, or the conversations I had with friends about course concepts. It wouldn't capture the mental effort I put in while working through problems on paper, or how I'd think about course material while doing mundane tasks. It also wouldn't show my stress levels, my competing responsibilities, or how hard I was actually trying despite my inconsistent visible presence. There's a whole person behind those login numbers.
